pi_items.source: Box & Jenkins (1976) airline dataset contains 12 years × 12 months monthly observations from 1949-1960. Verified by OLS regression analysis on the data in setup_task.sh.

OLS regression on log-transformed Box & Jenkins data with time trend and 11 seasonal dummies yields trend coefficient of 0.010069 (1.0069% monthly growth). This is consistent with the claimed value and within the specified tolerance.
Tolerance range [0.008, 0.012] is appropriate for the Box & Jenkins regression model, allowing for the actual coefficient of 0.010069.
Standard dummy variable specification for 12-month seasonality requires 11 dummy variables (one less than the number of seasons to avoid multicollinearity). This is correct for monthly data.
setup_task.sh line 16 explicitly creates the data file at this path. The file contains the Box & Jenkins airline passenger data.
export_result.sh line 9 and verifier.py line 56 both reference this path as the expected output file location where the regression analysis results should be saved.

pi_items.verified_value: Box, G. E. P., Jenkins, G. M. and Reinsel, G. C. (1976). Time Series Analysis, Forecasting and Control. Third Edition. Holden-Day. Monthly international airline passenger data, 1949-1960.
pi_items.source: setup_task.sh line 14-15 identifies source as 'Box & Jenkins, 1976'. Verification through web search and inspection of data values (112-622 range, 144 observations) confirms this is the canonical Box & Jenkins airline passengers dataset.

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the Box & Jenkins (1976) airline passenger dataset, a historical series of 144 monthly observations of international airline passengers from 1949-1960. The dataset is real (not synthetic). A log-transformed OLS regression with time trend and 11 seasonal dummy variables yields a trend coefficient of 0.010069 (representing ~1% monthly growth), which matches the metadata claim of 0.010 ± 0.002. The task requires creating seasonal dummies for 12 months (11 variables needed to avoid dummy trap) and performing a joint F-test on the dummy variables. The F-test yields F(11,131) = 59.05 with p-value < 1e-16, indicating strong seasonal significance.
